title: Magnetization-dependent and stacking-tunable Edelstein effect in two-dimensional magnet 2H-VTe2
abstract: The Edelstein effect in magnetic systems enables magnetization switching via the coupling between current-induced spin accumulation and intrinsic magnetic order, and is therefore highly promising for next-generation spintronic devices. Realizing and manipulating the Edelstein effect in two-dimensional (2D) magnetic systems is particularly desirable for achieving high-efficiency and multifunctional spintronic applications. In this work, based on first-principles calculations and symmetry analysis, we demonstrate that the Edelstein effect can intrinsically arise in the 2D in-plane ferromagnetic semiconductor 2H-VTe2, with its behavior strongly dependent on the magnetization orientation. For monolayer 2H-VTe2 with D3h crystal symmetry, under an applied current along the +x direction, only the time-reversal-even z component and the time-reversal-odd y(x) component of the spin accumulation are allowed when the magnetization is aligned along +x (+y). For ferromagnetic bilayer 2H-VTe2 in AB or BA stacking, where the crystal symmetry is reduced to C3v, additional spin components emerge with the presence of in-plane magnetization. Specifically, for magnetization along +x (+y), besides dSz_even and dSy_odd (dSz_even and dSx_odd), extra components such as dSy_even and dSz_odd (dSy_even) become allowed. Notably, these additional components can be reversibly switched by changing the stacking configuration from AB to BA via interlayer sliding. Our results not only deepen the understanding of current-induced spin accumulation in 2D magnetic systems from both symmetry and first-principles perspectives, but also identify 2H-MX2 materials as a promising platform for realizing intrinsic and tunable Edelstein effects in high-efficiency spin-orbit torque devices.
summary: This paper explores how the Edelstein effect in the 2D magnet 2H-VTe2 can be tuned by rotating magnetization or changing the stacking configuration of bilayers. It identifies a mechanism for nonvolatile control of spin-orbit torque through interlayer sliding. These findings suggest 2H-MX2 materials are promising for high-efficiency, multifunctional spintronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating how the Edelstein effect (current-induced spin accumulation) in 2D magnetic systems can be controlled via magnetization orientation and crystal symmetry (stacking configuration).
main_result: The study demonstrates that in 2H-VTe2, the Edelstein effect is magnetization-dependent and that additional spin components can be reversibly switched by changing the stacking from AB to BA via interlayer sliding.
method: First-principles calculations using Quantum Espresso, Wannier90, and WannierTools, with the Edelstein response tensor evaluated using the Kubo formula via the Linres code.
model_or_system: The system is the 2D in-plane ferromagnetic semiconductor 2H-VTe2, analyzed in both monolayer (D3h symmetry) and bilayer (C3v symmetry) configurations with AB and BA stacking.
key_observables: Edelstein response tensor (chi), spin accumulation components (delta S_x, delta S_y, delta S_z), and time-reversal-even/odd contributions.
important_parameters: Fermi energy (Ef), magnetization angle (phi), electric field (E), and Hubbard U = 1 eV.
main_assumptions: The reference electric field of 1 V/A used in calculations is noted as difficult to realize experimentally; calculations assume a specific thickness for monolayer and bilayer.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows atomic structure and band structure; Figures 2-3 show symmetry-allowed spin components for monolayers; Figures 4-6 show bilayer structures and stacking-dependent spin accumulation; Figures 7-11 show k-resolved spin accumulation and angular dependence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spintronic efficiency, presents the structural and electronic properties of 2H-VTe2, analyzes the monolayer symmetry and spin response, extends the analysis to bilayer stacking and its tunability, and concludes with the generalizability of the findings to other 2H-MX2 materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The atomic structure of monolayer 2H-VTe2. (b) The band structure of monolayer 2H-VTe2.

FIG. 2. Calculated symmetry-allowed current-induced spin accumulations in a unit cell as functions of Fermi energy in monolayer 2H-VTe2 under a reference electric field 1 V/˚Aalong +𝑥direction. (a) and (b) show the time-reversal-even (𝛿𝑆even 𝑧 ) and time-reversal-odd (𝛿𝑆odd 𝑦 ) components of spin accumulation when magnetization is along +𝑥direction. (c) and (d) show the time-reversal-even (𝛿𝑆even 𝑧 ) and time-reversal-odd (𝛿𝑆odd 𝑥 ) components of spin accumulation when magnetization is along +𝑦direction. The dashed blue region indicates the bandgap. Note that the magnitude of the electric field (1 V/˚A) is difficult to realize experimentally and is used only as a reference value.

FIG. 3. Angular-dependent spin accumulation in a unit cell of monolayer 2H-VTe2, which is denoted as a function of 𝜙(angle between magnetization direction and +𝑥direction). (a) denotes the symmetry-allowed time-reversal-even components (only 𝛿𝑆even 𝑧 here) of spin accumulation in a unit cell as function of 𝜙, while (b) denotes the symmetry-allowed time-reversal-odd components of spin accumulation in a unit cell as function of 𝜙. Note that the magnetization is rotating in plane, which means that 𝜙= 0 and 𝜙= 90◦denotes +𝑥and +𝑦magnetization, respectively. (c) and (d) show the time-reversal-even and time-reversal-odd components of spin accumulation when magnetization is along +𝑦direction.
